--- title: "Exploratory Data Analysis with Python" description: "A walkthrough of a simple EDA workflow using pandas and matplotlib." author: "Jonathan Pearce" date: "2026-02-15" image: thumbnail.png toc: true code-fold: true execute: echo: true warning: false --- ## Overview This example post demonstrates how a typical Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) blog post looks on *Casual Inference*. It generates synthetic data so the post renders without external dependencies. ## Generate Data ::: {#f1cfc61b .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt np.random.seed(123) n = 300 df = pd.DataFrame({ "age": np.random.randint(18, 65, n), "income": np.random.exponential(50000, n).round(2), "score": np.random.beta(2, 5, n).round(4), }) df.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
age income score
0 63 42639.29 0.0089
1 20 5414.64 0.4655
2 46 60170.99 0.2125
3 52 54112.52 0.1904
4 56 2517.17 0.2594
``` ::: ::: ## Distributions ::: {#cell-fig-distributions .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 3.5)) for ax, col in zip(axes, df.columns): ax.hist(df[col], bins=30, edgecolor="white") ax.set_title(col.capitalize()) fig.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Distribution of age, income, and score.](index_files/figure-html/fig-distributions-output-1.png){#fig-distributions} ::: ::: ## Correlations ::: {#3abd4dd6 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df.corr().round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
age income score
age 1.000 0.096 0.012
income 0.096 1.000 -0.077
score 0.012 -0.077 1.000
``` ::: ::: ## Next Steps - Handle outliers - Feature engineering - Modelling
